a8_personas.name: Convenience Chargers
a8_personas.description: Charge when convenient, no fixed routine. Most common group.

a8_personas.name: Opportunistic Chargers
a8_personas.description: Plug in whenever a power source is available regardless of battery level.

a8_personas.name: Power Users
a8_personas.description: High wattage chargers, old and new phones, heavy battery stress.

a8_personas.name: 20-80 Rule Followers
a8_personas.description: Charge between 20-85%, disconnect before full. Best battery health.

ai_observations: Nearly 95% of users fall into the low or medium risk categories for battery health, suggesting that the majority of young adults are managing their charging habits effectively.
Despite the popular advice to keep batteries between 20-80%, nearly 60% of young adults do not follow this practice, showing a clear gap between battery health awareness and daily behavior.
Overnight charging is surprisingly rare in this age group, with only 16 out of 222 respondents leaving their phones plugged in while they sleep.
The average user begins charging their phone when the battery drops to 20%, which aligns perfectly with the standard threshold recommended to prevent deep-discharge stress.
Device age has almost no impact on how often a person charges their phone, as indicated by the near-zero correlation between the two factors.
A small but distinct group of users—represented by cluster 3—waits until their battery hits 80% before plugging in, marking them as extreme high-threshold chargers.
Energy waste is significantly higher for the high-risk group, who lose an average of 0.0158 kWh compared to zero waste among those classified as low-risk.
The K-Means analysis reveals that users with newer phones, less than a year old, tend to charge twice as frequently as those with older devices.
A clear majority of users—61 out of 115—experience a very efficient battery drain rate of less than 5% per hour, indicating that most devices are operating under manageable loads.

Technical specifications like charger wattage show no statistically significant relationship with when a user decides to plug in their phone, suggesting personal habit drives behavior more than hardware.
Predictive modeling is highly accurate for this dataset, with a 97.8% success rate in classifying charging habits based on the available metrics.
Even among the highest battery drainers, only a small fraction of users experience a rapid loss of over 25% per hour, suggesting that most respondents have relatively healthy device performance.
